root_text.sn47.43:1.3: “ekamidāhaṁ, bhikkhave, samayaṁ uruvelāyaṁ viharāmi najjā nerañjarāya tīre ajapālanigrodhe paṭhamābhisambuddho.
root_text.sn47.43:1.4: Tassa mayhaṁ, bhikkhave, rahogatassa paṭisallīnassa evaṁ cetaso parivitakko udapādi:
root_text.sn47.43:1.5: ‘ekāyanvāyaṁ maggo sattānaṁ visuddhiyā sokaparidevānaṁ samatikkamāya dukkhadomanassānaṁ atthaṅgamāya ñāyassa adhigamāya nibbānassa sacchikiriyāya, yadidaṁ—cattāro satipaṭṭhānā.
root_text.sn47.43:2.2: Kāye vā bhikkhu kāyānupassī vihareyya ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;
root_text.sn47.43:2.5: dhammesu vā bhikkhu dhammānupassī vihareyya ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ.
root_text.sn47.43:2.6: Ekāyanvāyaṁ maggo sattānaṁ visuddhiyā sokaparidevānaṁ samatikkamāya dukkhadomanassānaṁ atthaṅgamāya ñāyassa adhigamāya nibbānassa sacchikiriyāya, yadidaṁ—cattāro satipaṭṭhānā’ti.
root_text.sn47.43:3.1: Atha kho, bhikkhave, brahmā sahampati mama cetasā cetoparivitakkamaññāya—seyyathāpi nāma balavā puriso samiñjitaṁ vā bāhaṁ pasāreyya, pasāritaṁ vā bāhaṁ samiñjeyya; evameva—brahmaloke antarahito mama purato pāturahosi.
root_text.sn47.43:3.2: Atha kho, bhikkhave, brahmā sahampati ekaṁsaṁ uttarāsaṅgaṁ karitvā yenāhaṁ tenañjaliṁ paṇāmetvā maṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn47.43:3.4: Ekāyanvāyaṁ, bhante, maggo sattānaṁ visuddhiyā sokaparidevānaṁ samatikkamāya dukkhadomanassānaṁ atthaṅgamāya ñāyassa adhigamāya nibbānassa sacchikiriyāya, yadidaṁ—cattāro satipaṭṭhānā.
root_text.sn47.43:4.2: Kāye vā, bhante, bhikkhu kāyānupassī vihareyya ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;
root_text.sn47.43:4.5: dhammesu vā, bhante, bhikkhu dhammānupassī vihareyya ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ.
root_text.sn47.43:4.6: Ekāyanvāyaṁ, bhante, maggo sattānaṁ visuddhiyā sokaparidevānaṁ samatikkamāya dukkhadomanassānaṁ atthaṅgamāya ñāyassa adhigamāya nibbānassa sacchikiriyāya, yadidaṁ—cattāro satipaṭṭhānā’ti.

translation_text.sn47.43:1.2: There the Buddha addressed the mendicants:
translation_text.sn47.43:1.3: “Mendicants, this one time, when I was first awakened, I was staying in Uruvelā at the goatherd’s banyan tree on the bank of the Nerañjarā River.
translation_text.sn47.43:1.4: As I was in private retreat this thought came to mind:
translation_text.sn47.43:1.5: ‘The four kinds of mindfulness meditation are the path to convergence. They are in order to purify sentient beings, to get past sorrow and crying, to make an end of pain and sadness, to discover the system, and to realize extinguishment.
translation_text.sn47.43:2.2: A mendicant would meditate observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.43:2.3: Or they’d meditate observing an aspect of feelings …
translation_text.sn47.43:2.5: or principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.43:2.6: The four kinds of mindfulness meditation are the path to convergence. They are in order to purify sentient beings, to get past sorrow and crying, to make an end of pain and sadness, to discover the system, and to realize extinguishment.’
translation_text.sn47.43:3.1: And then the divinity Sahampati, knowing my train of thought, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, vanished from the realm of divinity and reappeared in front of me.
translation_text.sn47.43:3.2: He arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his joined palms toward the Buddha, and said:
translation_text.sn47.43:3.3: ‘That’s so true, Blessed One! That’s so true, Holy One!
translation_text.sn47.43:3.4: Sir, the four kinds of mindfulness meditation are the path to convergence. They are in order to purify sentient beings, to get past sorrow and crying, to make an end of pain and sadness, to discover the system, and to realize extinguishment.
translation_text.sn47.43:4.2: A mendicant would meditate observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.43:4.3: Or they’d meditate observing an aspect of feelings …
translation_text.sn47.43:4.5: or principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.43:4.6: The four kinds of mindfulness meditation are the path to convergence. They are in order to purify sentient beings, to get past sorrow and crying, to make an end of pain and sadness, to discover the system, and to realize extinguishment.’
translation_text.sn47.43:5.1: That’s what the divinity Sahampati said.
translation_text.sn47.43:5.2: Then he went on to say:
translation_text.sn47.43:6.1: “The compassionate one, <j>who sees the ending of rebirth,
translation_text.sn47.43:6.3: By this path people crossed over before,
translation_text.sn47.43:6.4: will cross over, and are crossing over.’”
